section.title: The Birth of John and the Benedictus
section.sectionRationale: The promise made at the altar comes true. Elizabeth bears a son, and at his naming Zechariah insists, His name is John, and his long silence is broken. His first words are not complaint but prophecy: the Benedictus, a song blessing the God of Israel who has visited and redeemed His people, raised up a horn of salvation in David's house, and appointed this child to go before the Lord. It is one movement, from a birth that gathers the neighbors' joy to a father's Spirit-filled song of dawning salvation.
section.thesis: God keeps His word: John is born and named as commanded, and the freed tongue of his father blesses the God who has visited and redeemed His people, raising a horn of salvation in David's house and sending the forerunner before the dawning mercy of God.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the birth and naming of John; Zechariah's tongue loosed in praise; the Benedictus blessing the redeeming God; the child who will prepare the way; and John growing in the wilderness.

units.label: The birth and naming of John

verses.text: When the time came for Elizabeth to have her child, she gave birth to a son.
Her neighbors and relatives heard that the Lord had shown her great mercy, and they rejoiced with her.
On the eighth day, when they came to circumcise the child, they were going to name him after his father Zechariah.
But his mother replied, “No! He shall be called John.”
They said to her, “There is no one among your relatives who bears this name.”
So they made signs to his father to find out what he wanted to name the child.
Zechariah asked for a tablet and wrote, “His name is John.” And they were all amazed.

units.label: Zechariah's tongue loosed in praise
units.note: what then will this child become

verses.text: Immediately Zechariah’s mouth was opened and his tongue was released, and he began to speak, praising God.
All their neighbors were filled with awe, and people throughout the hill country of Judea were talking about these events.
And all who heard this wondered in their hearts and asked, “What then will this child become?” For the Lord’s hand was with him.

units.label: The Benedictus: the God who redeems
units.note: a horn of salvation in the house of David

verses.text: Then his father Zechariah was filled with the Holy Spirit and prophesied:
“Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel, because He has visited and {{lytrosis|redeemed}} His people.
He has raised up a {{keras|horn}} of salvation for us in the house of His servant David,
as He spoke through His holy prophets, those of ages past,
salvation from our enemies and from the hand of all who hate us,
to show mercy to our fathers and to remember His holy covenant,
the oath He swore to our father Abraham, to grant us
deliverance from hostile hands, that we may serve Him without fear,
in holiness and righteousness before Him all the days of our lives.

units.label: The child who will prepare the way
units.note: the tender mercy of our God

verses.text: And you, child, will be called a prophet of the Most High; for you will go on before the Lord to prepare the way for Him,
to give to His people the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins,
because of the {{splanchna|tender mercy}} of our God, by which the {{anatole|Dawn}} will visit us from on high,
to shine on those who live in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the path of peace.”

units.label: John grows in the wilderness
units.note: until his public appearance to Israel

verses.text: And the child grew and became strong in spirit; and he lived in the wilderness until the time of his public appearance to Israel.

lytrosis.gloss: Redemption, ransoming, deliverance by payment of a price. Zechariah blesses God because He has visited and worked redemption for His people. The word looks back to the exodus and forward to the greater redemption Christ will accomplish.

keras.gloss: Horn, a common biblical image of strength and victory, drawn from the horn of a powerful animal. A horn of salvation in David's house means a mighty Savior-King, fulfilling the promise of a strong deliverer from David's line.

splanchna.gloss: The inward parts, and so deep, gut-level compassion; here the tender mercy of God. It names the warm, heartfelt pity of God that moves Him to visit and save His people, the wellspring of the whole song.

anatole.gloss: The rising, as of the sun or a star, hence dawn or daybreak; the word can also mean a branch or shoot. Applied to Christ, it pictures the sunrise from heaven breaking on a world in darkness, bringing light and the path of peace.

basic.context: Elizabeth gives birth, and her neighbors rejoice at the Lord's mercy. At the boy's circumcision they assume he will be named after his father, but Elizabeth insists on John, and Zechariah, still mute, writes, His name is John. At once his speech returns and he praises God. Filled with the Holy Spirit, he prophesies the Benedictus: blessing the God of Israel who has visited and redeemed His people, raised a horn of salvation in David's house, and remembered His covenant with Abraham. Then he turns to his infant son, who will go before the Lord to prepare His way. John grows up in the wilderness until the day he appears to Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: The naming scene shows obedient faith: Zechariah does exactly what the angel commanded, and his tongue is freed, discipline giving way to praise. The Benedictus, like the Magnificat, is a mosaic of Old Testament promises, so it should be read as the voice of Israel's whole hope finding its answer. The key questions are what God has done in visiting His people and what role this child will play in the dawning salvation.
basic.originalLanguages: Zechariah blesses God who has worked lytrosis, redemption, for His people, raising a keras, a horn, of salvation in David's house. The song springs from the splanchna, the tender mercy, of God, by which the anatole, the Dawn or sunrise from on high, will visit those in darkness.
basic.typology: The horn of salvation from David's house fulfills God's covenant promise of a strong deliverer from David's line. John steps into the role of the prophet who prepares the Lord's way, and the rising Dawn from on high recalls Malachi's sun of righteousness, fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: Though the song blesses God the Father, its center is the coming Christ: the horn of salvation, the Lord whose way John prepares, and the Dawn from on high who brings light and peace. John's whole future is to point to Him.
basic.god: God is faithful to His covenant and His oath to Abraham, tender in mercy, mighty to redeem, and the giver of light and peace. He remembers, He visits, and He saves.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 132 promises a horn to sprout for David. Malachi 4 foretells the sun of righteousness rising with healing. Genesis 22 records the oath to Abraham. And Isaiah 9 speaks of light dawning on those in darkness.
basic.oneStory: God visits and redeems, and the forerunner is born to prepare the way. The Benedictus gathers up centuries of promise and announces that the dawn has come: the covenant is kept, salvation is near, and the path of peace is opening for a people long in darkness.

context.literary: This completes the John panel of the infancy narrative and answers the tension left hanging since verse 20: the mute priest speaks again. The birth scene and the Benedictus mirror the earlier promise, and the song forms a pair with the Magnificat, two Spirit-filled hymns that interpret the events. Verse 80 rounds off John's story until he reappears in chapter 3.
context.historical: Circumcision and naming took place on the eighth day. Children were commonly named after relatives, which is why the neighbors are surprised at John. Zechariah's use of a writing tablet reflects ordinary practice. The hill country of Judea buzzes with talk, showing how such a birth would be discussed in a close-knit community awaiting God's deliverance.
context.canonical: The Benedictus binds the covenant with Abraham, the promise to David, and the prophetic hope of a coming dawn into a single announcement of fulfillment. Its imagery of light shining in darkness and the path of peace threads through Luke and the New Testament, and its confidence that God has remembered His covenant frames the whole Gospel as the keeping of ancient promises.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God faithfully fulfilling His promise in John's birth and naming, to record Zechariah's Spirit-given prophecy interpreting these events as the dawn of redemption, and to define John's mission as the forerunner who prepares the Lord's way.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The events describe God's unique work at the threshold of the gospel. What endures is the model of obedient faith in Zechariah, the truth of God's covenant faithfulness, and the summons to walk in the light and peace that the Dawn from on high brings.

debates.question: Is the redemption of the Benedictus political or spiritual?

views.label: Rescue centered on forgiveness
views.case: Verse 77 defines salvation as the knowledge of salvation through the forgiveness of their sins, so the deliverance from enemies is ultimately from sin, death, and darkness, not merely Rome.

views.label: A whole-life deliverance
views.case: The song's language of enemies and serving God without fear includes the full restoration of God's people; yet its heart, as the song itself says, is forgiveness and the path of peace.

debates.bearing: Reading the redemption as centered on forgiveness guards against a merely political messiah and prepares for the cross, where the real enemies are overcome.

debates.question: Who is the Dawn or sunrise from on high?

views.label: The Messiah, Christ Himself
views.case: The rising light who visits from on high to shine on those in darkness and guide feet into peace is best read as Jesus, echoing Malachi's sun of righteousness.

views.label: The dawning of God's saving visitation
views.case: Some stress the event of God's salvation breaking like daybreak; even so, that visitation arrives in the person of Christ, so the readings meet.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage announces that God's light has come into the world's darkness in the coming Savior, and calls people to walk in His peace.

study.originalLanguages: Zechariah's song blesses the God of Israel because He has visited and worked lytrosis, redemption, for His people, the same word that recalls the ransom of the exodus. God has raised a keras, a horn, of salvation in the house of David, the horn being the ancient image of a strong deliverer, a mighty King. All of this flows from the splanchna, the deep, gut-level tender mercy of God, by which the anatole will visit from on high. That last word means the rising of the sun or a star, the daybreak, and it pictures Christ as the sunrise breaking over a world that sits in darkness and the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the path of peace. The vocabulary turns the whole song into a sunrise of hope: mercy, redemption, and light.

typology.type: The horn of salvation from David's house
typology.fulfillment: The promised strong deliverer from David's line, fulfilled in Jesus the Messiah-King, Psalm 132:17 and Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: The forerunner who prepares the Lord's way
typology.fulfillment: The prophet who readies the people for the Lord, fulfilled in John, Malachi 3:1 and Luke 3:4.

typology.type: The rising Dawn from on high
typology.fulfillment: The sun of righteousness who rises with healing, fulfilled in Christ the light of the world, Malachi 4:2 and John 8:12.

study.christ: The Benedictus blesses God the Father, but its whole horizon is the Son who is coming. He is the horn of salvation raised up in David's house, the Lord whose way John will prepare, and the Dawn from on high who shines on those in darkness and guides their feet into peace. The salvation He brings is defined as the forgiveness of sins, which points beyond any political rescue to the cross. John's greatness is entirely derivative: he is the prophet who goes before the face of the Lord, and the Lord is Jesus, in whom the tender mercy of God visits the world.
study.god: The God Zechariah blesses is the covenant-keeping God of Israel who does not forget. He remembers His holy covenant and the oath He swore to Abraham, and He acts out of tender, heartfelt mercy. He is mighty to redeem, raising a horn of salvation, and gentle as the dawn, bringing light to those in darkness and peace to troubled feet. He hears prayer, keeps His word across generations, and visits His people to save. The right response is the praise that pours from a freed tongue: Blessed be the Lord, the God of Israel.

crossReferences.note: God promises to make a horn sprout for David, the strong Savior-King echoed in the horn of salvation.
The sun of righteousness rising with healing in its wings, behind the image of the Dawn from on high.
The oath God swore to Abraham, remembered in the Benedictus as now being kept.
The people walking in darkness have seen a great light, fulfilled as the Dawn shines on those in the shadow of death.

study.oneStory: The forerunner is born, and his father's song declares that the long-promised redemption has dawned. God has kept His covenant with Abraham and His promise to David, raising a horn of salvation and sending the sunrise from on high. John will prepare the way; the Lord will bring the forgiveness of sins and the path of peace. The whole Old Testament hope now stands on tiptoe, waiting for the Savior who is about to be born.

sermon.title: Benedictus
sermon.why: Begg walks through Zechariah's prophecy in Luke 1:67-79, focusing on the source, reality, and purpose of God's promised salvation, how His mercy brings light into darkened hearts, forgiveness of sins, and true peace. A faithful, expository handling of the Benedictus at the center of this movement.

quiz.q: Why were the neighbors surprised at the naming of the child?

options.text: Because both parents insisted on John, though no relative bore that name.
options.note: Verses 60 to 63 show the surprise that they broke with family custom to obey the angel's command.

options.text: Because Elizabeth wanted to name him John rather than Zechariah.
options.note: True, but the fuller surprise was that no relative had the name and Zechariah agreed by writing.

options.text: Because they thought the child should have no name at all.
options.note: They intended to name him after his father; the issue was the specific name John.

options.text: Because John was a forbidden name in Israel.
options.note: The name was not forbidden; it simply was not in the family.

quiz.q: What happened when Zechariah wrote, His name is John?

options.text: His mouth was opened and his tongue released, and he began praising God.
options.note: Verse 64 records his speech restored the moment he obeyed.

options.text: The neighbors were all amazed at what had happened.
options.note: They were amazed, but the central event was Zechariah's speech returning as he praised God.

options.text: He remained silent for several more days.
options.note: His silence ended immediately, not later.

options.text: The child began to speak instead.
options.note: It was Zechariah, not the infant, whose speech was restored.

quiz.q: What does Zechariah bless God for in the Benedictus?

options.text: God has visited and redeemed His people, raising a horn of salvation in David's house.
options.note: Verses 68 and 69 are the heart of the song's blessing.

options.text: God has remembered His covenant and oath to Abraham.
options.note: True and part of the song, but the opening blessing centers on the redemption and the horn of salvation.

options.text: God has given Zechariah his voice back.
options.note: That prompted his praise, but the song blesses God for redeeming His people, not merely for the personal sign.

options.text: God has destroyed the Roman Empire.
options.note: The song speaks of salvation and forgiveness, not the overthrow of Rome.

quiz.q: What did Zechariah say his son John would do?

options.text: Go before the Lord to prepare His way and give His people the knowledge of salvation.
options.note: Verses 76 and 77 define John's mission as the forerunner.

options.text: Become a prophet of the Most High.
options.note: True, John is called a prophet, but his defining task was to prepare the Lord's way.

options.text: Reign as king over Israel.
options.note: John prepares the way for the King; he is not the King himself.

options.text: Serve as high priest in the temple.
options.note: John's calling was prophetic preparation, not the high priesthood.

quiz.q: How does the Benedictus picture the coming of the Savior?

options.text: As the Dawn from on high, shining on those in darkness and guiding their feet into peace.
options.note: Verses 78 and 79 use the image of sunrise breaking on the darkness.

options.text: As the tender mercy of God visiting His people.
options.note: True and stated, but the vivid picture is the Dawn rising to bring light and peace.

options.text: As a mighty army driving out Israel's foes.
options.note: The salvation is centered on forgiveness and peace, not a military campaign.

options.text: As a distant hope that may never arrive.
options.note: The song announces the dawn as breaking now; the hope is arriving, not fading.

questions: Zechariah's obedience, writing His name is John, freed his tongue for praise. Where might a simple act of obedience open the way to renewed joy in God for you?
The neighbors asked, What then will this child become? How does it steady you to know that the Lord's hand is at work shaping lives long before we see the outcome?
The Benedictus roots its confidence in a covenant God who remembers His promises. Which promise of God do you most need to remember today?
Zechariah names forgiveness of sins as the heart of salvation. How does keeping forgiveness central guard you from settling for smaller rescues than God offers?
Christ comes as the Dawn to those in darkness and the shadow of death. Where do you long for His light, and how can you walk toward the path of peace this week?

translationNotes.note: A horn of salvation uses the Old Testament image of a horn as a symbol of strength and victory; in David's house it means a mighty deliverer-King, echoing Psalm 132:17.

The Dawn renders a Greek word for the rising of the sun or a star; the BSB footnote gives the alternatives the Morning Light or the Sunrise. The word can also mean a branch, but the context of shining light favors the sunrise.
Became strong in spirit is noted by the source as read in some manuscripts as became strong in the Spirit; either way it marks God's hand preparing John for his prophetic calling.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 1:57-80. Heading, line break, note, and poem markers were skipped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage; none remain.
